# Button

Displays a button or an element styled like one.

## Contents

- [Installation](#installation)
- [Usage](#usage)
- [Variants](#variants)
- [Sizes and composition](#sizes-and-composition)
- [Processing composition](#processing-composition)
- [Recommendations](#recommendations)

## Installation

Add the Button component from the configured StackTrace registry.

```shell
npx shadcn-vue@latest add @stacktrace/button
```

## Usage

Use `Button` for actions performed in the current interface. Use `ButtonLink` when the interaction navigates through Inertia or sends an Inertia request. Choose a variant according to the action's importance and risk, not according to personal color preference.

## Variants

Variants communicate an action's priority and intent. Prefer the lowest-emphasis variant that still makes the action discoverable, and avoid placing several visually dominant actions in the same group.

### Default

Use the default variant for the primary action in a page, form, dialog, or action group—for example, *Save*, *Create*, or *Continue*. Prefer a single default button per action group so the next step remains obvious.

**Default example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button>Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Secondary

Use the secondary variant for a supporting action that should remain prominent but is less important than the primary action. It works well for a meaningful alternative beside a default button, but should not compete with it for attention.

**Secondary example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="secondary">Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Destructive

Use the destructive variant for actions that delete data, revoke access, or cause another difficult-to-reverse outcome. Do not use it merely to display an error or a negative status.

> **Confirm significant destructive actions: [Confirmation Dialog](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/confirmation-dialog)**
>
> Ask for confirmation when accidental activation would cause a significant or irreversible consequence. The confirmation should name the action and make its result clear.

**Destructive example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="destructive">Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Outline

Use the outline variant for neutral alternatives such as *Cancel*, *Back*, or an optional action beside the primary button. Its visible boundary is useful when the surrounding surface does not otherwise make a button obvious.

**Outline example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="outline">Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Ghost

Use the ghost variant for low-priority or frequently repeated actions in toolbars, table rows, menus, and compact controls. Use it only when the surrounding context already makes the element's interactivity clear.

**Ghost example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="ghost">Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Link

Use the link variant for a button action that should visually sit inside surrounding text. This variant changes appearance only. For actual Inertia navigation, use `ButtonLink variant="link"` so the element also has link behavior.

**Link example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="link">Button</Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

## Sizes and composition

Use the default size for most forms and dialogs, `sm` for dense interfaces such as toolbars and table rows, and `lg` for prominent standalone calls to action. Reserve icon sizes for icon-only controls.

> **Group related buttons: [Button Group](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/button-group)**
>
> When multiple related buttons form one compact control, use Button Group instead of manually joining their borders or spacing. Keep independent actions, such as Save and Cancel, as separate buttons.

### Icon

Use an icon-only button only when the symbol is widely understood in context, such as close, search, or next. Always provide an accessible name with `aria-label`. If the action could be ambiguous, include a visible text label instead.

**Icon example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="outline" size="icon" aria-label="Continue">
    <ChevronRightIcon />
  </Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
import { ChevronRightIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
</script>
```

### As child

Use `as-child` to apply button styling and behavior to another element without adding a wrapper. Preserve the semantics of the child element, and prefer `ButtonLink` instead when the child would be an Inertia link. Read the [composition fundamentals](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/fundamentals/composition) before building a custom component that accepts composed behavior.

**As child example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button as-child>
    <a href="/docs">Read the documentation</a>
  </Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

### Label and icon

Add an icon when it reinforces the label or improves recognition; do not use a decorative icon merely to make every button look busier. Compose icons and labels in the default slot. The button sizes nested SVG icons automatically, so manual width and height classes are normally unnecessary.

**Label and icon example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button variant="outline">
    <SaveIcon data-icon="inline-start" />
    Save
  </Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button } from '@/Components/Button'
import { SaveIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
</script>
```

### Inertia link

Use `ButtonLink` whenever a button-looking control navigates through Inertia or sends an Inertia request. It renders an Inertia `Link` with button styles and forwards props such as `method`, `data`, `preserve-scroll`, and `as`. During an active visit, a nested `ButtonState` responds directly to Inertia's `data-loading` attribute and displays its spinner automatically.

**Button link example**

```vue
<template>
  <ButtonLink href="/docs" variant="outline">
    Browse documentation
  </ButtonLink>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { ButtonLink } from '@/Components/Button'
</script>
```

Render non-GET actions as a button instead of an anchor.

```vue
<ButtonLink
  href="/logout"
  method="post"
  as="button"
  variant="outline"
>
  <ButtonState>Log out</ButtonState>
</ButtonLink>
```

## Processing composition

`Button` intentionally owns no asynchronous state. Compose `ButtonState` inside it when a button needs pending or recently successful feedback. Disable the outer button separately when repeated submissions should be prevented.

### Processing

Show processing feedback when an action takes long enough that the user may otherwise repeat it or wonder whether it started. For a regular button, bind the request state to `ButtonState` and disable the outer `Button` while processing to prevent duplicate submissions.

**Processing example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button
    variant="outline"
    :disabled="processing"
    @click="runTask"
  >
    <ButtonState :processing="processing">
      Run task
    </ButtonState>
  </Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button, ButtonState } from '@/Components/Button'
import { ref } from 'vue'

const processing = ref(false)

function runTask() {
  processing.value = true

  window.setTimeout(() => {
    processing.value = false
  }, 1200)
}
</script>
```

### Inertia form state

Bind an Inertia form's `processing` and `recentlySuccessful` values to `ButtonState`. The default slot is the idle content; use the `success` slot to customize the success label. Use recently successful feedback for a brief, local confirmation such as saving a field. Prefer a toast or persistent message when the outcome must remain visible after attention moves away from the button.

**Form state example**

```vue
<template>
  <Button
    variant="outline"
    :disabled="processing"
    @click="save"
  >
    <ButtonState
      :processing="processing"
      :recently-successful="recentlySuccessful"
    >
      <SaveIcon data-icon="inline-start" />
      Save

      <template #success>Saved</template>
    </ButtonState>
  </Button>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button, ButtonState } from '@/Components/Button'
import { SaveIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
import { ref } from 'vue'

const processing = ref(false)
const recentlySuccessful = ref(false)

function save() {
  if (processing.value || recentlySuccessful.value) {
    return
  }

  processing.value = true

  window.setTimeout(() => {
    processing.value = false
    recentlySuccessful.value = true

    window.setTimeout(() => {
      recentlySuccessful.value = false
    }, 1000)
  }, 1200)
}
</script>
```

```vue
<template>
  <form @submit.prevent="form.post('/projects')">
    <Button type="submit" :disabled="form.processing">
      <ButtonState
        :processing="form.processing"
        :recently-successful="form.recentlySuccessful"
      >
        <SaveIcon data-icon="inline-start" />
        Save

        <template #success>Saved</template>
      </ButtonState>
    </Button>
  </form>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Button, ButtonState } from '@/Components/Button'
import { useForm } from '@inertiajs/vue3'
import { SaveIcon } from '@lucide/vue'

const form = useForm({})
</script>
```

## Recommendations

### Icon direction markers

The `data-icon="inline-start"` and `data-icon="inline-end"` attributes are optional. Button sizing and spacing do not depend on them. If your application does not support right-to-left layouts or position-specific icon styling, you can omit these attributes. Keep them when you need a directional styling hook.

Hosted documentation: [Button](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/button)
